content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 10,
1993
Toni I.
Gallagher
34365 Dana Strand Road #3
Dana Point,
CA
92629
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-93—205
Dear Ms.
Gallagher:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard
ing your responsibilities as
a member of the Capistrano Bay Park
and Recreation District under the conflict—of—interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Since your advice
request concerns a series of future decisions,
we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.
2
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTIONS
AND
CONCLUSIONS
QUESTION 1.
Do you have a conflict of interest with respect to decisions
of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District concerning the
Dana Point Headlands Specific Plan by virtue of your condominium
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-93—205
Page
2
that is located within 300 feet of the boundaries of the specific
plan area?
CONCLUSION 1.
Because your property is within 300 feet of the proposed
Headland Specific Plan Area,
you may not participate in the
decision concerning the specific plan unless it will have no
financial effect on your real property.
QUESTION 2.
Do you have a conflict of interest with respect to decisions
of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District concerning the
Dana Point Headlands Specific Plan by virtue of your spouse’s
business interest which is operated from the condominium that is
located within 300 feet of the boundaries of the specific plan
area?
CONCLUSION 2.
If the decision will have a material financial effect on your
spouse’s business,
you may not participate in the decision.
Regulation 18702.2 provides that for a relatively small business
entity,
the effect of a decision is material where the decision
will result in:
(1)
an increase or decrease in the gross revenues
for a fiscal year,
or of the value of assets and liabilities of
$10,000 or more;
or
(2)
the business entity incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses
for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more.
QUESTION 3.
Does it make a difference if you are only voting on
recommendations of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District
to the Dana Point City Council?
CONCLUSION 3.
If you have a conflict of interest in a decision,
you are
prohibited from using your official position with the district to
influence
other agency.
This would include voting in your
official capacity to make a recommendation to another agency.
QUESTION 4.
If you do have a conflict of interest with respect to
decisions of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District
concerning the Dana Point Headlands Specific Plan,
are you
disqualified from any decision that affects the specific plan
area?


Our File No.
1—93—205
Page
3
CONCLUSION 4.
Generally each decision is analyzed separately to determine
if you have a conflict of interest.
You may participate in
decisions that will not have a material financial effect on your
interests so long as those deliberations do not result in a
reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which you were
disqualified.
This would include,
for example,
a decision on a
park to be located at the most distant end of the specific plan
area which might not affect you materially under Regulation
18702.3.
Decisions affecting the entire specific plan area will
continue to be a disqualifying economic interest.
QUESTION 5.
If you do have a conflict of interest with respect to
decisions of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District
concerning the Dana Point Headlands Specific Plan,
may you
participate as a member of a joint committee on city and park
issues that may involve discussion of the Dana Point Headlands
Specific Plan?
If you are elected as a Dana Point City
Councilmember,
would your disqualification obligations be
different?
CONCLUSION 5.
As a public official in any capacity you would be required to
disqualify yourself from any decision that will foreséeably and
materially financially affect your economic interests.
QUESTION 6.
May you appear before the Dana Point City Council to express
your views with respect to the Dana Point Headlands Project?
CONCLUSION 6.
So long as you do not purport to represent the district,
you
are not restricted in participating in proceedings before other
agencies in your private capacity.
QUESTION 7.
May you address the public on your views with respect to the
Dana Point Headlands Project?
CONCLUSION 7..
Regulation 18700.1(b) (2)
provides that where you communicate
with the general public or the press,
such communications are not
considered attempting to use your position to influence a
governmental decision and are not prohibited by the Act.


Our File No.
1-93—205
Page
4
FACTS
In 1992,
you were elected to the Capistrano Bay Park and
Recreation District
(the “district”).
The district is a special
district which is nat an agency of the City of Dana Point.
You own a condominium in a four—unit complex on Dana Strand
Road.
Your real property is located within 300 feet of the
Headlands Specific Plan,
a 121 acre area of currently undeveloped
land.
In addition,
your spouse operates an alarm business from
the condominium.
The City will be considering a variety of issues related to
the future use of the Headlands area of the city.
These decisions
include zoning and land use within the specific plan area,
and
consideration of permits and environmental impact reports.
ANALYSIS
I.
Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District
The Act was adopted by the voters of the State of California
by initiative in 1974.
The purpose for the conflict—of-interest
provisions of the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether
elected or appointed,
would perform their duties in an impartial
manner,
free from bias caused by their own financial interests or
the financial interests of persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in mak
ing,
or otherwise using the official’s position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
A “public official” is defined in Section 82048 and
Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is:a member, officer,
employee,
or consultant of a state or local government agency.
This definition would include a member of the Capistrano Bay Park
and Recreation District.
A.
Economic Interests
Section 87103 provides:
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has
a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.


Our File No.
1—93—205
Page
5
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
***
For purposes of this section,
indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official,
the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
•indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103(a)—(d).
You have a variety of interests that may be affected by
district decisions.
First,
your condominium is within the
district and within 300 feet of the specific plan area in
question.
(Section 87103(b).)
Additionally,
your spouse’s
business is operated from the condominium.
(Section 87103(a)
and
(d).)
We assume that your interests in the condominium and the
business are greater than $1,000.
Thus,
you are prohibited from
participating in any decision that will have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on your condominium or
your spouse’s business.
B.
Foreseeability and Materiality
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
1—93—205
Page
6
The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether an effect is material,
depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision.
For example, where an economic
interest,
such as your condominium or spouse’s business, is
directly involved in a decision of the district, Regulation
18702.1(a)
(copy enclosed)
provides that generally, the effect of
the decision is deemed material and disqualification is required.
Regulation 18702.3
(copy enclosed)
provides in relevant part
that the effect of a decision on real property in which an
official has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest,
is material if:
The real property in which the official has an
interest,
or any part of that real property,
is
located within a 300 foot radius of the boundaries
(or proposed boundaries)
of the property which is
the subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the official’s
real property interests.
Regulation 18702.3(a) (1)
Your property is within 300 feet of the proposed Headland
Specific Plan Area that is the subject of your question.
Since
your real property is in such close proximity to the property
which is the subject of the decision,
you may not participate in
the decision concerning the specific plan unless it will have no
financial effect on your real property.
3
Your spouse’s business will also be indirectly affected by
the decision.
4
To determine whether your spouse’s business will
be materially affected,
you would apply Regulation 18702.2
(copy
enclosed).
However,
since a material financial effect on any one
economic interest will result in your disqualification we have not
gone on to further analyze your spouse’s business.
The financial effect may be an increase or decrease in the
value of real property.
A conflict of interest would exist where
your property is financially affected whether you voted for your
interests or against them.
“Disqualification is required before a
vote
(or any other action to influence the decision)
is undertaken
in order to assure complete confidence in the impartiality of
government decisions.”
(Dickerson Advice Letter,
No.
A-84-090;
Stehle Advice Letter,
No.
A-81--08-094.)
The business is considered directly involved in a decision when
the business:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the decision
will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal,
or similar
request or;
(2)
Is a named party in the proceeding;
or
(3)
Is the
subject of the proceeding because the decision involves the
issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit,
or other entitlement to,
or contract with, the business.


Our File No.
1—93—205
Page
7
The Commission cannot determine whether there will be a
material financial effect on your property.
However,
we have
advised that if an appraisal is performed by a person qualified to
perform the appraisal,
and it determines,
based on the
Commission’s materiality regulations, that decisions will not
materially financial effect the official’s property.,
the official
may rely on the determination.
(Stone Advice Letter,
No.
A—92-
133a.)
C. Participation
The terms “making, participating in making,
and influencing”
have been broadly interpreted consistent with the policies and
purposes of the Act.
1.
A public official makes a decision if the official votes
on a matter; appoints a person; obligates or commits the
official’s agency to any course of action; or, enters into any
contractual agreement on behalf of the agency.
(Regulation
18700(b),
copy enclosed.)
2.
A public official “participates in the making of a
governmental decision” when,
acting within the authority of the
official’s position, the official negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or private person
regarding the decision;
or advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker,
either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review,
by conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of judgment on the part
of the official and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision; or preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or
opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is
to influence the decision.
(Regulation 18700(c).)
3.
With regard to a governmental decision which is within
or before an official’s agency or an agency appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of the agency, the official is
attempting to use the official’s position to influence the
decision if,
for the purpose of influencing the decision,
the
official contacts,
or appears before,
or otherwise attempts to
influence,
any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.
Attempts to influence include,
but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client,
or customer.
(Regulation 18700.1(a),
copy
enclosed.)
With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an agency not covered by subsection (a), the official is
attempting to use the official’s position to influence the
decision if,
for the purpose of influencing the decision,
the
official acts or purports to act on behalf of,
or as the
representative of, the official’s agency to any member,
officer,


Our File No.
1-93—205
Page
8
employee or consultant of an agency.
Such actions include,
but
are not limited to the use of official stationery.
(Regulation
18700.1(c).)
Thus,
when any decision regarding the specific plan is
considered by the district,
you must abstain from any
participation in the item.
This is the case even where the
district only makes
a recommendation to the Dana Point City
Council,
and the city council will make the ultimate
determination.
Pursuant to the regulation you are prohibited from
influencing the city council in your official capacity.
Where you
vote for the recommendation,
you would be voting in your official
capacity to influence the city council.
(Blegan Advice Letter,
No.
A—85—176,
copy enclosed.)
D.
SeqInentation
You have also asked if,
due to your property interests,
you
are disqualified as to
the decisions concerning the specific
plan area.
Clearly,
where a decision applies to the specific plan
generally,
such as changing boundaries,
the entire specific plan
area is considered to be the property that is the subject to the
decision and you may not participate unless there is no financial
effect.
Moreover,
in some cases a series of decisions will be too
interrelated to be considered separately.
(Miller Advice Letter,
No.
A—82—l19.)
For example, if a decision concerninga road is
presented to the district,
and the alternate route is in an area
within 300 feet of your real property,
you would not be able to
participate as to either decision.
(Nord Advice Letter,
No.
A-82-
038.)
This is because the determination regarding the more
distant site will effectively, determine the use of the route near
your property for which you would be disqualified.
Where the
decisions are too interrelated to be decided separately,
you must
disqualify yourself as to all the decisions under consideration.
(Scher Advice Letter,
No.
A-88-479.)
In most other cases,
each governmental decision is analyzed
independently to determine if there will be a foreseeable material
financial effect on an official’s financial interest.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77..)
Thus, if a decision concerned a road
2,000 feet from the nearest boundary of your property,
and this
decision was not interrelated to decisions concerning other,
closer .portions of the specific plan,
you may participate so long
as the decision will not affect the value of the condominium by
$10,000 or more or will affect the rental value by $1,000 or more
in a 12-month period.
Where other decisions concerning the specific plan are
separable, the following procedure should be followed to permit
you to participate:


Our File No.
1-93—205
Page
9
1.
The decisions in which you have a disqualifying financial
interest should be segregated from the other decisions.
2.
The decisions from which you are disqualified should be
considered first,
and a final decision reached bythe district
without you participating in any way.
3.
Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
decision in which you have a disqualifying interest,
you may
participate in the deliberations regarding other portions of the
decision,
so long as those deliberations do not result in a
reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which you were
disqualified.
(Huffaker Advice Letter,
No. A-86-343.)
E.
Representing Your
Own Interests Before Your Agency.
As noted above, participation in governmental decisions has
been interpreted broadly in furtherance of the goals of the Act.
However, Regulation 18700.1(b)
expressly provides an exception
where an otherwise disqualified official appears in the same
manner as any other member of the general public before the
official’s own agency in the course of its prescribed governmental
function to represent the official’s own personal interests in
real property.
Where the exception does apply,
your comments must be limited
to your personal interests,
and care should be taken to clarify
that you are not acting in any official capacity.
(Larsen Advice
Letter,
No.
A—87-15l.)
Thus,
the exception would not permit you
to appear before the district to represent any other person’s
interests or the interests of any group.
II.
Appearances Before the Dana Point City Council
Regulation 18700.1 provides:
(a)
With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an official’s agency or
an agency appointed by or sublect to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if,
for the purpose of
influencing the decision,
the official contacts,
or
appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.
Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official
5
..
In addition, Regulation 18700.1(b) (2)
provides that where you
communicate with the general public or the press,
such
communications are not considered attempting to use your position
to influence a governmental decision.


Our File No.
1-93—205
Page
10
on behalf of a business entity, client,
or
customer.
***
Cc)
With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an agency not covered by
subsection (a), the official is attempting to use
his or her official position to influence the
decision if,
for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official acts or purports to act on
behalf of,
or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer,
employee or
consultant of an agency.
Such actions include, but
are not limited to the use of official stationery.
Regulation 18700.1 provides that even conduct in your private
capacity may be characterized as using your official position to
influence a decision.
However, since the city council is not
under the budgetary control of your agency,
you are not restricted
in participating beföre the city council in your private capacity.
However,
you are prohibited from purporting to represent the
district or acting in an official capacity. ( e.g.,
Stout
Advice Letter,
No.
1-88—313; Hineline Advice letter,
No.
A—88-
149.)
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.6
6
Sincerely
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0c per page.
Jeff Narschner
al Counsel
John W. Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division

that is located within 300 feet of the boundaries of the specific
plan area?

decision concerning the specific plan unless it will have no

QUESTION 2.

may you

QUESTION 6.

CONCLUSION 6.

are not restricted in participating in proceedings before other

May you address the public on your views with respect to the

CONCLUSION 7..

with the general public or the press,

Our File No.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: AND

CONCLUSION: QUESTION 1.
Do you have a conflict of interest with respect to decisions
of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District concerning the
Dana Point Headlands Specific Plan by virtue of your condominium
et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-93—205
that is located within 300 feet of the boundaries of the specific
plan area?
CONCLUSION 1.
Because your property is within 300 feet of the proposed
Headland Specific Plan Area,
you may not participate in the
decision concerning the specific plan unless it will have no
financial effect on your real property.
QUESTION 2.
Do you have a conflict of interest with respect to decisions
of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District concerning the
Dana Point Headlands Specific Plan by virtue of your spouse’s
business interest which is operated from the condominium that is
located within 300 feet of the boundaries of the specific plan
area?
CONCLUSION 2.
If the decision will have a material financial effect on your
spouse’s business,
you may not participate in the decision.
Regulation 18702.2 provides that for a relatively small business
entity,
the effect of a decision is material where the decision
will result in:
(1)
an increase or decrease in the gross revenues
for a fiscal year,
or of the value of assets and liabilities of
$10,000 or more;
or
(2)
the business entity incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses
for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more.
QUESTION 3.
Does it make a difference if you are only voting on
recommendations of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District
to the Dana Point City Council?
CONCLUSION 3.
If you have a conflict of interest in a decision,
you are
prohibited from using your official position with the district to
influence
other agency.
This would include voting in your
official capacity to make a recommendation to another agency.
QUESTION 4.
If you do have a conflict of interest with respect to
decisions of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District
concerning the Dana Point Headlands Specific Plan,
are you
disqualified from any decision that affects the specific plan
area?


Our File No.
1—93—205
CONCLUSION 4.
Generally each decision is analyzed separately to determine
if you have a conflict of interest.
You may participate in
decisions that will not have a material financial effect on your
interests so long as those deliberations do not result in a
reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which you were
disqualified.
This would include,
for example,
a decision on a
park to be located at the most distant end of the specific plan
area which might not affect you materially under Regulation
18702.3.
Decisions affecting the entire specific plan area will
continue to be a disqualifying economic interest.
QUESTION 5.
If you do have a conflict of interest with respect to
decisions of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District
concerning the Dana Point Headlands Specific Plan,
may you
participate as a member of a joint committee on city and park
issues that may involve discussion of the Dana Point Headlands
Specific Plan?
If you are elected as a Dana Point City
Councilmember,
would your disqualification obligations be
different?
CONCLUSION 5.
As a public official in any capacity you would be required to
disqualify yourself from any decision that will foreséeably and
materially financially affect your economic interests.
QUESTION 6.
May you appear before the Dana Point City Council to express
your views with respect to the Dana Point Headlands Project?
CONCLUSION 6.
So long as you do not purport to represent the district,
you
are not restricted in participating in proceedings before other
agencies in your private capacity.
QUESTION 7.
May you address the public on your views with respect to the
Dana Point Headlands Project?
CONCLUSION 7..
Regulation 18700.1(b) (2)
provides that where you communicate
with the general public or the press,
such communications are not
considered attempting to use your position to influence a
governmental decision and are not prohibited by the Act.


Our File No.
1-93—205
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission June 10, 1993 Toni I. Gallagher 34365 Dana Strand Road #3 Dana Point, CA 92629 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1-93—205 Dear Ms. Gallagher: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard ing your responsibilities as a member of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District under the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 Since your advice request concerns a series of future decisions, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. 2 Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS QUESTION 1. Do you have a conflict of interest with respect to decisions of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District concerning the Dana Point Headlands Specific Plan by virtue of your condominium 1 Government Code Sections 81000—91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. 1-93—205 Page 2 that is located within 300 feet of the boundaries of the specific plan area? CONCLUSION 1. Because your property is within 300 feet of the proposed Headland Specific Plan Area, you may not participate in the decision concerning the specific plan unless it will have no financial effect on your real property. QUESTION 2. Do you have a conflict of interest with respect to decisions of the Capistrano Bay Park and Recreation District concerning the Dana Point Headlands Specific Plan by virtue of your spouse’s business interest which is operated from the condominium that is located within 300 feet of the boundaries of the specific plan area? CONCLUSION 2. If the decision will have a material financial effect on your spouse’s business, you may not participate in the decision. Regulation 18702.2 provides that for a relatively small business entity, the effect of a decision is material where the decision will result in: (1) an increase or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year, or of the value of assets and liabilities of $10,000 or more; or (2) the business entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more.
